Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: We are going to break down the problem into several steps Step 1 For each case order the events by timestamp and then aggregate the activities into a sequence as a string of comma separated activities Step 2 Group by the sequence string to get each variant and count the number of cases per variant Step 3 Determine the top K variants Step 4 We need to get the list of case ids that belong to the top K variants Then we will filter the original event log to only include events from those cases

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We can use a common table expression or subqueries First we create a subquery that for each case we get the ordered sequence of activities and then we group by that sequence to get the count of cases per variant We can use STRING AGG with ORDER BY

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note the problem says ordered sequence of activities that occur in a single case determined by sorting events within that case by their timestamp So this is correct

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then we want to group by the sequence and count the cases per variant Then we want to get the top K variants by frequency We can use a parameter for K Then we need to get the case ids that have a sequence in the top k variants Then finally we select all events from the original event log where the case id is in qualifying cases

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: But there is a caveat the sequence is a string What if two different sequences become the same string For example if an activity contains a comma Then we might have a problem

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: DuckDB supports arrays We can do ARRAY AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity array Then we can group by the array

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The array comparison in DuckDB is element wise and order matters because we ordered by timestamp So it should be correct

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Considering the problem lets assume activities do not contain commas We will use the comma separated string However there is a better way we can avoid building the entire string and instead use arrays Lets try with arrays

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: WITH case sequences AS SELECT case id ARRAY AGG activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity array FROM event log GROUP BY case id Then in variant counts SELECT activity array COUNT star AS frequency FROM case sequences GROUP BY activity array Then top k variants SELECT activity array FROM variant counts ORDER BY frequency DESC LIMIT k Then qualifying cases SELECT cs case id FROM case sequences cs WHERE EXISTS SELECT 1 FROM top k variants t WHERE t activity array equals cs activity array

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets test with a small example event log case 1 has A B case 2 has A B case 3 has A C Then variant counts gives AB to 2 and AC to 1 If k equals 1 then top k variants is only AB Then qualifying cases are case 1 and 2 Then we return all events for case 1 and 2 This is correct

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: One more issue the timestamp might have ties The problem does not specify We must break ties Because if two events in the same case have the same timestamp then the order is not defined

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: We will add a secondary sort by activity to ensure determinism We choose ORDER BY timestamp activity

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: What if there are ties in frequency Then we take the first K rows arbitrarily The problem does not specify how to handle ties

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: We will break ties by the lexicographical order of the activity array using ORDER BY frequency DESC activity array LIMIT k

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Final query uses case sequences variant counts top k variants qualifying cases and then selects all rows from event log where case id is in qualifying cases ordered by case id timestamp activity
